title: What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)?
content:
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is an AI framework that enhances the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) by combining them with external knowledge retrieval. Instead of relying solely on the model's pre-trained knowledge, RAG first retrieves relevant documents from a knowledge base and then uses those documents as context for generating more accurate and grounded responses.

The RAG pipeline typically consists of three main stages: indexing, retrieval, and generation. During indexing, documents are chunked into smaller segments, converted into vector embeddings using an embedding model, and stored in a vector database. During retrieval, a user query is embedded using the same model and compared against the stored vectors to find the most semantically similar documents. During generation, the retrieved documents are combined with the original query into a prompt that is fed to an LLM for answer generation.

RAG has become the standard approach for building AI-powered question answering systems, chatbots, and knowledge assistants because it allows models to access up-to-date, domain-specific information without requiring expensive fine-tuning. Organizations use RAG to build internal knowledge bases, customer support bots, document search systems, and compliance assistants.

title: Understanding Vector Databases
content:
Vector databases are specialized database systems designed to efficiently store, index, and query high-dimensional vector embeddings. Unlike traditional relational databases that work with structured rows and columns, vector databases operate on dense numerical representations of data, enabling similarity-based search rather than exact keyword matching.

At their core, vector databases use approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) algorithms to find vectors that are closest to a given query vector in high-dimensional space. Popular ANN algorithms include HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World), IVF (Inverted File Index), and product quantization techniques. These algorithms trade a small amount of accuracy for dramatic improvements in search speed, making it possible to search through billions of vectors in milliseconds.

Vector databases are essential infrastructure for modern AI applications. They serve as the retrieval layer for RAG systems, enable semantic search across documents and products, power recommendation engines based on content similarity, and provide long-term memory for AI agents. Key features to look for in a vector database include high query throughput, support for metadata filtering, scalability to large datasets, and flexible deployment options.

title: Endee: High-Performance Vector Database for AI
content:
Endee is an open-source, high-performance vector database built specifically for AI search and retrieval workloads. It is implemented in C++ and optimized for modern CPU architectures including AVX2, AVX512, NEON, and SVE2, enabling it to handle up to 1 billion vectors on a single node with exceptional performance.

Endee supports both dense vector search and sparse retrieval capabilities for hybrid search use cases. It includes payload filtering for metadata-aware retrieval, backup workflows for data safety, and flexible deployment options including Docker containers and local builds. The database exposes an HTTP API and provides official client libraries for Python, Node.js, Java, and Go.

Key use cases for Endee include building RAG pipelines for question answering and chat assistants, implementing semantic search for documents and products, creating recommendation and matching systems, and serving as long-term memory for AI agents. Endee combines the simplicity of a purpose-built vector database with the performance characteristics needed for production AI workloads.

title: How Embedding Models Work
content:
Embedding models are neural networks that transform text, images, or other data types into fixed-dimensional numerical vectors (embeddings) that capture semantic meaning. When two pieces of text have similar meanings, their embedding vectors will be close together in the vector space, even if they use completely different words.

Modern text embedding models are typically based on transformer architectures. Models like Sentence-BERT (SBERT) and its variants (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2) are trained using contrastive learning objectives that bring semantically similar sentence pairs closer together while pushing dissimilar pairs apart. These models produce embeddings of various dimensions — 384, 768, or 1024 dimensions are common — with higher dimensions generally capturing more nuanced semantic information at the cost of increased storage and computation.

The choice of embedding model significantly impacts the quality of downstream applications like search and RAG. Key factors include the model's training data domain, embedding dimensionality, inference speed, and multilingual capabilities. For production systems, lightweight models like all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (384 dimensions) offer an excellent balance of quality, speed, and resource efficiency, making them popular choices for real-time applications.

title: Semantic Search vs Keyword Search
content:
Keyword search and semantic search represent two fundamentally different approaches to information retrieval. Keyword search (also called lexical search) matches documents based on the exact occurrence of query terms, using techniques like TF-IDF or BM25 scoring. It is fast and predictable but fails when users express the same concept using different words — a problem known as the vocabulary mismatch problem.

Semantic search, on the other hand, uses vector embeddings to understand the meaning behind queries and documents. By converting both queries and documents into dense vectors in a shared embedding space, semantic search can find relevant results even when there is no word overlap between the query and the document. For example, a search for 'automobile maintenance tips' can successfully retrieve a document about 'car repair guides' because the embedding model understands they refer to the same concept.

Hybrid search combines both approaches, using keyword matching for precision and semantic search for recall. Many modern search systems use a two-stage retrieval pipeline: a fast first stage using sparse retrieval (like BM25) to generate a broad candidate set, followed by a re-ranking stage using dense embeddings to sort results by semantic relevance. This approach leverages the strengths of both methods for optimal search quality.

title: Building Production RAG Pipelines
content:
Building a production-grade RAG pipeline requires careful attention to each stage of the process: document processing, embedding generation, vector storage, retrieval, and answer generation. Each stage has important design decisions that directly impact the quality and reliability of the final system.

Document chunking strategy is one of the most critical decisions. Chunks that are too large may dilute relevant information with noise, while chunks that are too small may lose important context. Common approaches include fixed-size chunking with overlap (typically 512 characters with 50-100 character overlap), semantic chunking that splits at paragraph or section boundaries, and recursive chunking that tries multiple separators. The optimal chunk size depends on the embedding model's context window and the typical query patterns.

Retrieval quality can be improved through several techniques: using metadata filtering to narrow the search space before vector similarity search, implementing hybrid retrieval that combines dense and sparse methods, applying re-ranking models to improve result ordering, and using query expansion or hypothetical document generation to improve query representations. For the generation stage, prompt engineering is crucial — include clear instructions, relevant context, and source attribution requirements to ensure the LLM produces grounded, accurate responses.

title: Transformer Architecture Explained
content:
The Transformer architecture, introduced in the 2017 paper 'Attention Is All You Need' by Vaswani et al., revolutionized natural language processing and has become the foundation for most modern AI models. The key innovation is the self-attention mechanism, which allows the model to weigh the importance of different parts of the input sequence when producing each output element.

A standard Transformer consists of an encoder and a decoder, each made up of multiple identical layers. Each encoder layer contains a multi-head self-attention mechanism followed by a position-wise feed-forward network, with residual connections and layer normalization around each sub-layer. The decoder is similar but includes an additional cross-attention layer that attends to the encoder output.

Encoder-only models (like BERT) are used for understanding tasks such as classification and embedding generation. Decoder-only models (like GPT) are used for text generation tasks. Encoder-decoder models (like T5 and BART) are used for sequence-to-sequence tasks like translation and summarization. The Transformer's ability to process sequences in parallel (unlike RNNs) and capture long-range dependencies through attention has made it the dominant architecture in AI.

title: Fine-Tuning Large Language Models
content:
Fine-tuning is the process of adapting a pre-trained large language model to perform well on specific tasks or domains. While pre-trained models have broad general knowledge, fine-tuning allows them to specialize in particular areas, follow specific instruction formats, or align with human preferences.

There are several approaches to fine-tuning. Full fine-tuning updates all model parameters but requires significant computational resources. Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) and QLoRA (Quantized LoRA) modify only a small fraction of the model's parameters, dramatically reducing memory and compute requirements while maintaining most of the performance gains. Instruction tuning trains models on collections of tasks formatted as instructions, improving their ability to follow diverse commands.

RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) is another fine-tuning technique where human evaluators rank model outputs, and those rankings are used to train a reward model that guides further training. This approach was critical in creating models like ChatGPT and Claude that are helpful, harmless, and honest. For domain-specific applications, fine-tuning on high-quality domain data (such as medical, legal, or technical texts) can significantly improve performance compared to using a general-purpose model.

title: AI Agents and Agentic Workflows
content:
AI agents are autonomous systems that use large language models to reason, plan, and take actions to accomplish goals. Unlike simple chatbots that respond to individual queries, agents can break down complex tasks into steps, use tools and APIs, maintain memory across interactions, and iterate on their approach based on intermediate results.

The core components of an AI agent include: a foundation model for reasoning and planning, a memory system (often powered by vector databases like Endee) for storing and retrieving past observations and context, a set of tools the agent can invoke (such as web search, code execution, or database queries), and an orchestration loop that manages the plan-act-observe cycle.

Popular frameworks for building AI agents include LangChain, LlamaIndex, CrewAI, and AutoGen. These frameworks provide abstractions for tool calling, memory management, multi-agent collaboration, and workflow orchestration. Vector databases play a crucial role in agent architectures by serving as the long-term memory store — agents can save important observations, tool outputs, and conversation history as vectors, then retrieve relevant context mid-execution to inform their next actions.

title: Cosine Similarity in Vector Search
content:
Cosine similarity is the most widely used distance metric in vector search and embedding-based retrieval systems. It measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors, producing a value between -1 and 1, where 1 indicates identical direction (maximum similarity), 0 indicates orthogonality (no similarity), and -1 indicates opposite direction.

The formula for cosine similarity between two vectors A and B is: cos(θ) = (A · B) / (||A|| × ||B||), where A · B is the dot product and ||A|| and ||B|| are the magnitudes (L2 norms) of the vectors. The key advantage of cosine similarity over Euclidean distance is that it is invariant to vector magnitude — it only considers the direction (angle) of the vectors, not their length.

In practice, when vectors are normalized to unit length (magnitude of 1), cosine similarity becomes equivalent to the dot product, which can be computed very efficiently using SIMD instructions on modern CPUs. This is why many vector databases, including Endee, offer precision options like INT8 quantization — normalized vectors can be quantized with minimal loss of ranking quality, dramatically reducing memory usage and increasing search throughput for large-scale deployments.

title: Prompt Engineering for RAG Systems
content:
Prompt engineering is the practice of designing and optimizing the text prompts given to large language models to elicit the best possible responses. In RAG systems, prompt engineering is particularly important because the prompt must effectively combine the user's question with the retrieved context documents to produce accurate, grounded answers.

A well-designed RAG prompt typically includes several components: a system instruction that defines the model's role and behavior, the retrieved context documents clearly labeled with source information, the user's original question, and explicit instructions about how to use the context. Key best practices include instructing the model to only answer based on the provided context, asking it to cite specific sources, and telling it to acknowledge when the context is insufficient to answer a question.

Advanced prompt engineering techniques for RAG include chain-of-thought prompting (asking the model to reason step by step), few-shot prompting (providing example question-answer pairs), and self-consistency (generating multiple answers and selecting the most common one). The prompt format should also account for the context window limitations of the LLM — if retrieved documents are too long, they may need to be truncated or summarized before being included in the prompt.

title: Document Chunking Strategies
content:
Document chunking is the process of splitting large documents into smaller segments before generating embeddings and storing them in a vector database. The choice of chunking strategy significantly impacts retrieval quality, as it determines the granularity at which information can be found and the amount of context available in each retrieved result.

Fixed-size chunking is the simplest approach, splitting text into segments of a predetermined character or token count with optional overlap between consecutive chunks. Overlap (typically 10-20% of the chunk size) ensures that information near chunk boundaries is captured in at least one chunk. This method is easy to implement and works well for homogeneous text like articles and reports.

More sophisticated approaches include semantic chunking (using sentence or paragraph boundaries), recursive chunking (trying multiple separators like double newlines, single newlines, sentences, and words in order), and structure-aware chunking (preserving document hierarchy like headers, sections, and subsections). For specialized content types like code, HTML, or markdown, language-aware parsers can split content at meaningful boundaries. The optimal chunk size depends on the embedding model's maximum input length, the typical query length, and the density of information in the source documents.

title: FastAPI for AI Application Backends
content:
FastAPI is a modern, high-performance Python web framework designed for building APIs. It has become the de facto standard for building backends for AI and machine learning applications due to its speed, automatic documentation generation, and native support for async operations that are crucial for handling AI inference workloads.

Key features that make FastAPI ideal for AI backends include automatic OpenAPI (Swagger) documentation generation from Python type hints and Pydantic models, built-in request validation and serialization, native async/await support for handling concurrent requests during model inference, dependency injection for managing model instances and database connections, and WebSocket support for streaming responses.

A typical AI application backend built with FastAPI includes routes for health checks, model inference endpoints, data ingestion pipelines, and configuration management. FastAPI's integration with Pydantic provides robust request/response validation, ensuring that API consumers receive clear error messages and that internal services receive correctly typed data. For production deployments, FastAPI is typically served with Uvicorn (ASGI server) behind a reverse proxy like Nginx, with Docker containers for consistent deployment environments.

title: HNSW: The Algorithm Behind Vector Search
content:
Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) is the most widely used algorithm for approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search in vector databases. It was introduced by Yuri Malkov and Dmitry Yashunin in 2016 and provides an excellent balance of search speed, recall quality, and index construction time.

HNSW works by building a multi-layered graph structure where each layer is a navigable small-world graph. The top layer contains a sparse subset of all data points, and each successive layer includes more points, with the bottom layer containing all points. During search, the algorithm starts at the top layer and greedily navigates to the nearest neighbor at each layer, using the result as the entry point for the next layer. This hierarchical approach allows the algorithm to take large jumps in the vector space at higher layers and progressively refine the search at lower layers.

Key parameters for HNSW include M (the number of bi-directional links per element, affecting the graph density and search quality), ef_construction (the size of the dynamic candidate list during index building, affecting build quality), and ef_search (the size of the candidate list during search, trading speed for recall). Higher values of these parameters generally improve recall at the cost of memory usage and search time. HNSW indexes can be further optimized with product quantization or scalar quantization to reduce memory footprint while maintaining search quality.

title: Evaluation Metrics for RAG Systems
content:
Evaluating RAG systems requires assessing both the retrieval and generation components separately as well as the end-to-end pipeline. Several metrics and frameworks have been developed specifically for this purpose.

For retrieval evaluation, standard information retrieval metrics include: Recall@K (the fraction of relevant documents found in the top K results), Precision@K (the fraction of top K results that are relevant), Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR, the average of the reciprocal ranks of the first relevant result), and Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (nDCG, which accounts for the position of relevant results). These metrics require ground truth relevance labels, which can be manually annotated or generated using LLM-as-judge approaches.

For generation evaluation, key metrics include faithfulness (whether the generated answer is supported by the retrieved context), answer relevancy (whether the answer addresses the question), and hallucination rate (how often the model generates information not present in the context). Frameworks like RAGAS (Retrieval Augmented Generation Assessment) and DeepEval provide automated pipelines for computing these metrics. End-to-end metrics such as answer correctness and user satisfaction can be measured through A/B testing and user feedback collection in production systems.
